source_metadata.title_raw: Heather M. Minner - A-19-205 - October 28, 2019 - Cupertino

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Heather M. Minner 
City Attorney 
City of Cupertino10300 Torre Ave 
Cupertino, CA 95014-3255 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-205 
Dear Ms. Minner: 
October 28, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
· 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May the. Mayor of Cupertino take part in decisions pertaining to the replacement of City 
Hall and the expansion of a library, given that his residence is within 500 feet of both project sites? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, the Mayor may take part in decisions related to the City Hall replacement and library 
expansion projects because clear and convincing evidence indicates that neither project would·have 
a measurable impact on his property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-205 
Page No. 2 
Two projects have been proposed in the City of Cupertino: the replacement of the existing 
City Hall and the expansion of the Library. 
The City Hall replacement project proposes to demolish the existing City Hall building and 
construct a new City Hall building in the same general location of the existing City Hall. The new 
City Hall building would be two stories tall and approximately 40,000 square feet in size, with both 
the ground floor and second floor each providing approximately 20,000 square feet of floor area. 
The City Hall building would be built atop one level of below grade parking. The below grade 
parking garage would measure approximately 47,000 square feet in size and would provide up to 
118 parking spaces. The proposed City Hall Replacement would accommodate a staff of 
approximately 1-02 persons, allowing for the hiring of ten additional City Hall-employees, or 11 
percent future growth. The addition of underground parking would absorb the parking impact of 
additional employees. The maximum height of the new City Hall building would be approximately 
45 feet. 
The Library expansion project proposes to expand the ground floor of the existing library by 
approximately 2,000 square feet to accommodate additional seating for events and ancillary 
facilities such as restrooms and a small lobby. This would be accomplished by constructing a new 
Program Room addition to the existing building that can seat up to 130 people. The Program Room 
would only be used periodically for large events. The proposed expansion would occur on the south 
side of the Library. The proposed Program Room is planned to be approximately centered between 
the east and west wings of the existing Library building. 
Steven Scharf, the Mayor of Cupertino, owns a home located within 500 feet of the Library 
building. His home is also located between 500 and 1,000 feet of the existing City Hall building, 
though a small portion of the City Hall parking lot is within 500 feet of his residence. His 
residential real property is a 1,937 square-foot conventional two-story home, located in a 
homogenous neighborhood with roughly several hundred similar hom~s extending in all directions. 
, There are no commercial uses in the neighborhood blocks immediately surrounding Mayor Scharfs 
property. However, as noted, the Cupertino Civic Center Plaza, which includes City Hall, the 
Library (i.e., the locations of the proposed projects), and recreational playing fields, is located to the 
west of the neighborhood, approximately 500 feet from Mayor Scharfs property. 
The Mayor's property is located on a quiet neighborhood street that connects to two other 
similarly quiet neighborhood streets, and none of those streets provides direct access to the City 
Hall or Library buildings. The Mayor's property is separated from the City Hall and Library by-
among other things-· a parking lot, a field, a creek bed, and several residential homes. Neither the 
City Hall Replacement nor the Library Expansion Project would be visible from the Mayor's 
residence. 
According to the Civic Center Master Plan and its accompanying transit analysis, 
implementation of the City Hall Replacement and/or Library Expansion Projects would have a less-
than-significant impact on surrounding roadways. The Master Plan acknowledged the possibility of 
fugitive dust air quality impacts during project construction, but determined that such impacts 
would be less than significant with the proposed construction mitigation measures. Additionally, the 


File No. A-19-205 
Page No. 3 
Master Plan determined that "construction noise levels at the residences along Farallone Drive are 
expected to be ... below the significance threshold." The Mayor's property is further buffered from 
construction noise by being one house removed from the far side of Farallone Drive. 
Your request for advice also included: (1) an aerial city map of the mayor's house and the 
location of City Hall and the Library; and (2) an appraisal report prepared for a different potential 
conflict involving a proposed "Regnart Creek Trail Project."2 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." "A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family," or on certain economic interests, including "[a]ny real property in which the 
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." 
(Section 87103.) 
When a public official's economic interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision, 
Regulation 18701 ( a) provides that "[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a 
proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any 
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract, with the financial interest, including any 
decision affecting a property interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l )-(6)." (Regulation 
18701 (a).) However, in this instance, Mayor Scharf s real property is not explicitly involved in the 
decision. Thus, Regulation 18701(b) provides the standard for determining whether a decision's 
financial effect on Mayor Scharf s real property-is reasonably foreseeable. Regulation 18701 (b) 
provides, "[a] financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, 
if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." However, "[i]f the financial result cannot be expected 
absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable." 
Regulation 18702.2 provides materiality standards for determining when a reasonably 
foreseeable effect on an interest in real property is material. Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) provides that 
the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in 
which an official has a financial interest, other than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the 
decision involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there 
is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the 
official's property. Regulation 18702.2(a)(8) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
2 In Minner Advice Letter, No. A-I 9-109, we advised that clear and convincing evidence indicated that decisions 
related to the Regnart Creek Trail Project would have no measurable impact on the Mayor's residential real property, 
despite the Mayor's home being located 290 feet from the nearest edge of the Trail Project. 


File No. A-19-205 
Page No. 4 
effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial 
interest, other than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the decision involves property located 
more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of the parcel, and the decision 
would change the parcel's development potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, 
character (by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality), or market value. 
Here, you have noted that Mayor Scharf owns real property within 500 feet of the Library. 
You have also noted the Mayor's residence is between 500 and 1,000 feet of the existing City Hall 
building, and a small portion of the City Hall parking lot is within 500 feet of his residence. As the 
language of Regulation 18702.2 indicates, the relevant distance for purposes of applying the 
regulation is generally the distance from parcel-to-parcel, not the distance from building-to-
building. Additionally, we must consider the impact that the City Hall Replacement Project may (or 
may not) have on nearby parking, which further puts the City Hall parking lot at issue. Accordingly, 
for decisions related to the City Hall Replacement Project, as well as decisions related to the , 
Library Expansion Project, Regulation l 8702.2(a)(7) is applicable. Because each set of decisions 
involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of Mayor Scharfs real property, 
any foreseeable financial effect on his real property is presumably material unless there is clear and 
convincing evidence that the decision(s) will not have any measurable impact on the Mayor's 
property. 
Based on the facts provided, it is evident that none of the decisions related to either project 
would change the development potential or highest and best use of Mayor Schatfs property. With 
regard to the property's character, the distance and physical barriers between the residence and the 
project sites indicate that neither project would impact traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, 
or privacy. Additionally, the Civic Center Master Plan and its accompanying analysis concluded 
that the projects would have a less-than-significant impact on surrounding roadways, less than 
significant impact on air quality with the proposed construction mitigation measures, and 
construction noise levels are expected to be "below the significance threshold" for houses even 
closer than the Mayor's. 
Additionally, the facts indicate that neither project would impact the market value or income 
producing potential of the Mayor's real property. In addition to the lack of impacts noted above, the 
projects can fairly be characterized as merely improving upon buildings and services already in 
place. This is in contrast to other advice letters, such as the Wright Advice Letter, No. A-17-201, 
where approximately 23 acres of undeveloped land would be developed for commercial and 
residential use. There, we wrote, "[a] reasonably prudent person would believe, based on the 
significant change in use of the undeveloped property, that the decision would affect the market 
value of surrounding properties, even those 700 feet away." Here, however, that rationale is 
inapplicable and it is not reasonably foreseeable that the mere improvement of the City Hall and 
Library would affect the market value or income producing potential of Mayor Scharf s real 
property, which is separated from the project sites by the physical barriers noted _above. 
Accordingly, we conclude that clear and convincing evidence indicates that the decisions 
related to either project would not have a measurable impact on the Mayor's real property. Mayor 
Scharf s real property does not present a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act, and he may 
take part in the decisions relating to both projects. 


FileNo. A-19-205 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:sal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

Hall and the expansion of a library, given that his residence is within 500 feet of both project sites?

CONCLUSION: Yes, the Mayor may take part in decisions related to the City Hall replacement and library 
expansion projects because clear and convincing evidence indicates that neither project would·have 
a measurable impact on his property.
